items.question: Explain k-fold cross-validation and how the final score is computed.
items.answer: Split labeled data into k disjoint folds. For each fold i, train on the other k−1 folds and evaluate on fold i, recording metric mᵢ. The CV score is typically mean(m₁…mₖ); std across mᵢ indicates stability. Each sample is validated exactly once.

items.question: When do you use StratifiedKFold instead of KFold?
items.answer: Use StratifiedKFold for classification—especially imbalanced classes—so each fold preserves global class proportions. Plain KFold can leave rare classes out of a validation fold, making metrics like recall or PR-AUC undefined or noisy for that round.

items.question: Why must preprocessing live inside the CV loop?
items.answer: Fitting a scaler or feature selector on all of X before splitting leaks validation-fold statistics into training. Each fold must fit transforms on training indices only and apply them to the validation fold—usually via a sklearn Pipeline with cross_val_score.

items.question: What is nested cross-validation and why is it needed?
items.answer: Nested CV uses an inner CV loop on each outer training set to select hyperparameters or models, then scores on the outer held-out fold. This prevents optimistic bias from using the same CV scores both to tune and to report final performance.
